original_abstract: **Introduction:** Dietary medicinal plants have emerged as a focal point in alternative medicine research, primarily due to their dual role in disease prevention and therapeutic intervention. **Aim:** This study was designed to characterize the polyphenolic profiles of several indigenous plant extracts—*Mentha longifolia, M. arvensis, Tinospora cordifolia, Cymbopogon citratus, Foeniculum vulgare, Cassia absus, Camellia sinensis*, and *Trachyspermum ammi*—and subsequently evaluate their antioxidant potential, cytotoxicity, antimicrobial efficacy, and enzyme inhibitory activities. **Methods:** Antioxidant capacity was measured using DPPH, superoxide, hydroxyl, and nitric oxide radical scavenging assays. Antidiabetic potential was assessed via enzymatic inhibition, while anticancer and antibacterial activities were determined using the MTT assay and disc diffusion methods, respectively. **Results:** The medicinal plant polyphenolic extracts (MPPE) demonstrated robust antioxidant activity across all assays, a result highly correlated with their elevated total phenolic and flavonoid contents. UHPLC analysis identified 25 distinct polyphenolic compounds across the eight species, classified primarily as phenolic acids, flavonoids, and alkaloids. Notably, 3-feroylquinic acid was the most abundant compound (1,302 mg/L), with significant concentrations of rosmarinic acid, chlorogenic acid, and rutin (560–780 mg/L) found in *M. longifolia, C. absus*, and *C. sinensis*. While *C. sinensis* exhibited the highest overall phenolic density—surpassing other species by 20–116%—*T. cordifolia* was uniquely distinguished by its high alkaloid content. Biological assays revealed that *T. ammi* and *C. citratus* possessed the highest cytotoxicity against Caco-2 cells, whereas *M. arvensis* and *F. vulgare* were potent enzyme inhibitors. Furthermore, *F. vulgare* and *C. sinensis* showed significant antibacterial activity against pathogens including *S. aureus* and *E. coli*. **Discussion:** Principal component analysis (PCA) confirmed distinct clustering of the extracts based on their specific functional properties. These findings validate the therapeutic potential of these indigenous species, highlighting them as rich reservoirs of phytogenic compounds that warrant further exploration for modern pharmacological applications.
humanized_abstract: Medicinal plants constitute an important proportion of dietary substances being investigated for their potential application in alternate medicine. Thus, the objective of this work was to assess the polyphenolic constituents, in-vitro antioxidant activity, cytotoxicity along with antimicrobial efficacy of some indigenous plant extracts evaluated through various bioassays. Extracts of eight medicinal plants—Mentha longifolia (M. L) and Mentha arvensis (M. A), Tinospora cordifolia (T. C), Cymbopogon citratus (C. C), Foeniculum vulgare (F. V), Cassia absus (C. A) and Camellia sinensis (C. S), Trachyspermum ammi (T. A)—were analyzed for the evaluation of the efficacy of plant extracts. Free radical scavenging assays such as DPPH, superoxide scavenging, hydroxyl scavenging assays, nitric oxide scavenging assay were done in order to check the in-vitro antioxidant potential of the MPPE. Also enzyme inhibition tests like amylase inhibition and protein phosphatase inhibition were carried out in order to study the possible anti-diabetic properties of medicinal plant extracts. Along with this, the cytotoxic effect of MPPE on Caco-2 cell lines and antibacterial effects on certain microbial strains were assessed through MTT assay and disk diffusion method, respectively. MPPE revealed a strong free radical scavenging activity (at all concentrations tested) along with total phenolic content and total flavonoid content (TFC) values and was highly correlated with each other (r = 0.9899, p = 0.002). Also, 25 compounds representing phenolic acids, flavonoids and alkaloids were detected using UHPLC-Q-Orbitrap-Fourier Transform Mass Spectroscopy (FT-MS) in the selected species with 3-feroylquinic acid as the dominant compound (1302 mg/L). This study reveals a positive correlation of MPPE antioxidants activity with respect to their TPC and TFC content. Species-wise identification of major bioactive compound such as *M.M. longifolia*, *C. absus* and *C. S.* having rosmarinic acid (560 mg/L), chlorogenic acid (780 mg/L) and rutin (720 mg/L), respectively. However, *C. S.* exhibits highest phenolic density (34.44 × 10(3) mg/100g) by having 20, 59 and 116 % increased phenolic density values when compared with other five medicinal plant extracts. *T. cordifolia* was having an unique characteristic by exhibiting highest density of alkaloids (14.31 × 10(3) mg/100g) when compared with other species. *T. ammi* and *C. citratus* exhibited enhanced cytotoxic effect against Caco-2 cells lines whereas *M. arvensis* and *F. vulgare* exhibited pronounced enzyme inhibiting properties. In addition, *F. vulgare* and *C. S.* demonstrated effective antibacterial effects against S. aureus (25 mm) and E. coli (18 mm) respectively. Using PCA, a separation of herbal plants was clearly seen based on their unique and desired activity. Thus this study validated various applications of native plant species due to their large number of phytogenic compounds and enhances their application towards formulation of numerous pharmacological drugs.
